title: Sexual Offences Act 1967 partly decriminalises sex between men
description: The Sexual Offences Act 1967 partly decriminalised private sexual activity between two men in England and Wales, provided both were 21 or older. Public acts and any involving more than two people remained offences.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Stonewall Riots energise LGBT activism internationally
description: Sustained protest at the Stonewall Inn in New York marked a visible rise in LGBT activism in the United States that re-energised campaigners in the UK and elsewhere.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: North-Western campaign becomes Campaign for Homosexual Equality
description: The North-Western Homosexual Law Reform Committee, active in the decriminalisation campaign, renamed itself the Campaign for Homosexual Equality and grew into a national organisation.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Campaign for Homosexual Law Reform founded in Ireland
description: The Campaign for Homosexual Law Reform began in the Republic of Ireland. Its founding members included David Norris, later a senator, alongside future Irish presidents Mary McAleese and Mary Robinson.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK Gay Liberation Front active 1970 to 1973
description: The UK Gay Liberation Front drew together a diverse group of activists pursuing a liberationist agenda, in contrast to the more reformist Campaign for Homosexual Equality. It operated until 1973.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Gay Liberation Front disrupts the Festival of Light
description: The first event of the church-based anti-gay morality campaign known as the Festival of Light was disrupted by activists from the Gay Liberation Front.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: First London Pride march held
description: The first London Pride march took place on 1 July with over 2,000 participants.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland holds its first Pride demonstration
description: On 27 June around ten people marched from the Department of Justice at St Stephen's Green to the British Embassy in Merrion Square, Dublin, protesting the criminalisation of homosexuality in Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Gay Working People's Campaign meetings in Leeds
description: Members of the AEEU, EEPTU and TGWU trade unions held several meetings in Leeds in May. A follow-up meeting in February 1976 agreed a Charter for Gay Workers.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Hirschfeld Centre opens in Dublin
description: The Hirschfeld Centre opened in Dublin as headquarters for the National Gay Federation and as a safe space, event venue and community resource for the queer community.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: International Lesbian and Gay Association formed in Coventry
description: The International Lesbian and Gay Association was formed at a fringe meeting in Coventry. It is now known as the International Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Trans and Intersex Association.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Homosexuality decriminalised in Scotland
description: Scottish law was brought into line with England and Wales, decriminalising private sex between two men aged 21 and over.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: First National Gay Conference held in Cork
description: The first National Gay Conference took place over three days in May at Connolly Hall in Cork, addressing the situation of gay men and lesbians across Ireland and encouraging solidarity between them.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Homosexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 1982
description: Male homosexual activity was decriminalised in Northern Ireland following a ruling by the European Court of Human Rights.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Murder of Declan Flynn in Dublin
description: Declan Flynn, a gay man, was murdered on 10 September in Fairview Park, Dublin. The killing became a focal point for outrage over anti-LGBT violence in Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Fairview Park protest march against anti-LGBT violence
description: In March a mass protest was held in Fairview Park, Dublin, following the murder of Declan Flynn, drawing attention to violence against the LGBT community.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Homophobic campaign targets Tatchell in Bermondsey by-election
description: Labour candidate Peter Tatchell lost the Bermondsey by-election amid a sustained homophobic campaign. He was physically attacked and received death threats.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Lesbian and Gay Employment Rights group founded
description: Lesbian and Gay Employment Rights (LaGER) was founded and recruited Shane Enright of the TGWU as a gay male worker representative.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: NALGO adopts lesbian and gay rights motion
description: The union NALGO passed a motion declaring that lesbian and gay rights were as much a trade union issue as any other area of discrimination, strongly backed by the TGWU delegation.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Lesbians and Gays Support the Miners formed
description: Dai Donovan of the TGWU travelled to London with a message of solidarity, and Lesbians and Gays Support the Miners was invited to the Dulais valley in Wales during the miners' strike.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TGWU includes lesbian and gay issues in equality training
description: At the TUC Congress, TGWU regional equality courses led by Diana Holland, national officer for women, race and equalities, began including lesbian and gay issues in training.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Homosexuality decriminalised on Guernsey
description: Sexual activity between men was decriminalised on Guernsey, a Channel Island crown protectorate.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Betty Gallacher defends a colleague living with AIDS
description: TGWU member Betty Gallacher successfully defended a colleague living with AIDS from others who wanted him sacked, by directly educating the workplace membership. She also came out at a bus conference and raised funds for a member's bereaved family.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Lesbians and Gays Support the Printworkers formed
description: Lesbians and Gays Support the Printworkers was formed to back print workers sacked by newspaper owner Rupert Murdoch during the Wapping dispute. Some of its members were print workers themselves.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Irish Congress of Trade Unions publishes workplace guidelines
description: The Irish Congress of Trade Unions published Lesbian and Gay Rights in the Workplace: Guidelines for Negotiators.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Hirschfeld Centre in Dublin destroyed by fire
description: The Hirschfeld Centre in Dublin was destroyed by a fire believed to have been hate-inspired arson.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Princess Diana opens first specialist HIV and AIDS ward
description: Diana, Princess of Wales, opened the first specialist HIV-positive ward and pointedly shook hands with patients without wearing protective gloves or a mask, challenging stigma around the disease.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Section 28 of the Local Government Act 1988 enacted
description: Section 28 of the Local Government Act 1988 forbade the so-called promotion of homosexuality in schools and other services. The measure prompted renewed LGBT activism.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Kiss-in protest outside Leinster House in Dublin
description: Activists staged a kiss-in outside Leinster House in Dublin at a time when sexual activity between men was still illegal, taking a considerable personal risk. A later protest was held outside the Vatican Embassy.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Gay and Lesbian Equality Network founded in Ireland
description: The Gay and Lesbian Equality Network was founded by Don Donnelly, Charles Kerrigan, Suzy Byrne, Kieran Rose and Christopher Robson to change legislation and social policy toward full equality and protection for LGBT people in Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: European court rules in Norris v Ireland
description: David Norris lost his domestic case for same-sex rights, but the European Court of Human Rights case Norris v Ireland paved the way for the later decriminalisation of homosexuality in Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Prohibition of Incitement to Hatred Act 1989 in Ireland
description: The Prohibition of Incitement to Hatred Act 1989 outlawed incitement to hatred on grounds including sexual orientation in the Republic of Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Lesbians and Gays in MSF group launched
description: Led by Lesley Mansell, a self-organised LGBT group launched within the MSF union and became an advisory subcommittee to the union's executive.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TGWU conference backs support for lesbian and gay members
description: At the 1989 TGWU conference, Shane Enright proposed a motion, seconded by Alan Scott, calling on the union to support lesbian and gay members. It led directly to inclusion in the union's structures.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Stonewall UK set up to oppose Section 28
description: The campaign group Stonewall was established to oppose Section 28 and other barriers to equality.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: OutRage! direct-action group founded
description: The direct-action group OutRage! was founded by Peter Tatchell and others. It continued campaigning until 2011.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: World Health Organisation removes homosexuality from disease list
description: The World Health Organisation removed homosexuality from its classification of diseases. The date, 17 May, is now marked annually as the International Day Against Homophobia, Biphobia and Transphobia.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: First Belfast Pride held in Northern Ireland
description: The first Pride event in Northern Ireland took place in Belfast, growing from around 50 participants at its first parade.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TUC begins Lesbian and Gay Rights at Work seminars
description: The TUC began annual Lesbian and Gay Rights at Work seminars, chaired by Ken Gill and Anne Gibson, with Diana Holland and Phillip Inglesant among the key speakers.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: MSF launches member-staffed LGBT+ helpline
description: The MSF union launched an LGBT+ helpline staffed by its own members.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Homosexuality decriminalised on the Isle of Man
description: The Isle of Man aligned its law with mainland UK, decriminalising sexual activity between men.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Homosexuality decriminalised in the Republic of Ireland
description: Sexual activity between men was decriminalised in the Republic of Ireland, the result of a long campaign by David Norris and the Campaign for Homosexual Law Reform.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland equalises age of consent and opens military service
description: Following decriminalisation, the Republic of Ireland equalised the age of consent, and LGBT people could serve openly in the armed forces.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TGWU publishes Pride Over Prejudice leaflet
description: The TGWU published the Pride Over Prejudice leaflet supporting LGBT members, who began self-organising and addressing workplace issues such as equal benefits for same-sex partners.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK lowers age of consent for men to 18
description: The age of consent for sexual activity between men in the UK was lowered from 21 to 18.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TUC and Stonewall launch For Richer or Poorer campaign
description: The TUC and Stonewall launched the For Richer or Poorer leaflet and lobbied for equal marriage for same-sex couples.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TUC runs Shop the Bad Boss reporting hotline
description: The TUC worked with the Pink Paper to run a three-day hotline for LGBT readers to report poor employment practices.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TUC votes to hold annual Lesbian and Gay conference
description: The TUC Congress voted to hold an annual Lesbian and Gay conference, which came to include bisexual and trans members in later years.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland outlaws workplace discrimination by sexual orientation
description: The Employment Equality Act 1998, followed by the Equal Status Act 2000, outlawed discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation in the Republic of Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: First TUC Lesbian and Gay Conference and LGBT officer
description: The TUC held the first of many annual Lesbian and Gay conferences of trade union delegates and appointed Peter Purton as officer for LGBT workers. Bisexual and trans members were included by 2002.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Northern Ireland gender reassignment employment protections
description: The Sex Discrimination (Gender Reassignment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1999 provided a degree of protection for trans people undergoing gender reassignment in employment and vocational training.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK lifts ban on gay and bisexual people in the military
description: Gay and bisexual people were allowed to serve in the UK armed forces.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Schools OUT! relaunched to challenge homophobia in education
description: Schools OUT! was relaunched with Sue Sanders as co-chair to challenge homophobia, biphobia and transphobia in the public sector and support LGBT education in the curriculum. It launched the first UK LGBT History Month in 2004.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK equalises the age of consent at 16
description: The age of consent for sex between men was lowered to 16 in the UK, equalising it with the age for heterosexual activity.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Same-sex couples allowed to adopt in England and Wales
description: Same-sex couples were legally allowed to adopt, with no exemptions for religious agencies. The law applied to England and Wales and was recognised in Northern Ireland and Scotland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK government states transsexualism is not a mental illness
description: The Lord Chancellor's Office published Government Policy Concerning Transsexual People, stating categorically that transsexualism is not a mental illness.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Employment Equality (Sexual Orientation) Regulations 2003
description: The regulations put comprehensive workplace protection in place for all sexual orientations for the first time in England, Wales and Scotland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Section 28 repealed across the UK
description: The repeal of Section 28 of the Local Government Act 1988 removed the prohibition on the so-called promotion of homosexuality in schools and services.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Unions challenge religious exemptions in the Amicus case
description: Seven unions took a High Court case challenging religious exemptions in the Employment Equality (Sexual Orientation) Regulations 2003. Although the case was lost, the exemption was narrowed to those in religious orders rather than general employees of religious organisations.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Gender Recognition Act 2004 enables legal transition
description: The Gender Recognition Act enabled adults in the UK to transition legally and be issued new birth certificates, with protection for their previous identity. The process was heavily medicalised.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Civil Partnership Act 2004 introduces same-sex partnerships
description: The Civil Partnership Act 2004 introduced legal recognition for same-sex couples. Civil partnerships became available from 2005 in England and Wales, with Scotland and Northern Ireland following.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK Black Pride founded
description: UK Black Pride began as a day trip to Southend-on-Sea by members of the online network Black Lesbians in the UK. Co-founder Phyllis Akua Opoku-Gyimah, known as Lady Phyll, is a trade union and human rights activist.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Betty's Bus becomes a fixture at London Pride
description: Betty's Bus, driven by TGWU member Betty Gallacher, became a cornerstone for trade union participation at London Pride for 19 years.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Equality Act 2006 extends consumer protection
description: The Equality Act 2006 extended protection in the provision of goods and services to all sexual orientations in England, Wales and Scotland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Isle of Man bars dismissal for sexual orientation
description: The Employment Act 2006 made it unlawful to dismiss employees on the grounds of their sexual orientation on the Isle of Man.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Age of consent equalised in Jersey and the Isle of Man
description: The age of consent for same-sex activity was equalised at 16 in Jersey and the Isle of Man.
region: Channel Islands and Isle of Man

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Amicus and TGWU merge to form Unite the Union
description: The Amicus and TGWU unions merged to form Unite the Union, with equality structures enshrined in rule at the heart of the new organisation.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Age of consent equalised in Northern Ireland
description: The UK Parliament passed the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 to equalise the age of consent in Northern Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Isle of Man introduces legal gender recognition
description: The Gender Recognition Act 2009 allowed transgender people on the Isle of Man to change their gender with legal recognition.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Equality Act 2010 consolidates UK equality law
description: The Equality Act 2010 consolidated existing equality law and gave the LGBT+ community standing equivalent to other groups facing discrimination. It applied to England, Wales and Scotland, with differences in Northern Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland introduces civil partnerships
description: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 enabled same-sex couples to enter civil partnerships in the Republic of Ireland. The scheme ended in 2015 when access to marriage was equalised.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Peter Tatchell Foundation established
description: The Peter Tatchell Foundation was founded to work on human rights and to assist LGBT+ migrants to the UK.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Isle of Man introduces civil partnerships and adoption
description: The Civil Partnership Act 2011 introduced civil partnerships on the Isle of Man and also provided for adoption by same-sex couples.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: First openly LGBT members elected to the Dail
description: Jerry Buttimer, John Lyons and Dominic Hannigan took their seats as the first openly LGBT members of the Dail Eireann, representing Cork South-Central, Dublin North-West and Meath East. Katherine Zappone became the first openly lesbian senator.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Jersey allows civil partnerships in churches and adoption
description: From April, same-sex couples in Jersey could register a civil partnership in churches that agreed to it, and could also legally adopt children.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Northern Ireland court legalises same-sex couple adoption
description: Adoption by unmarried and same-sex couples was legalised after rulings in Northern Ireland, confirmed by the Court of Appeal in 2013.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Marriage (Same Sex Couples) Act 2013 legalises equal marriage
description: The Marriage (Same Sex Couples) Act 2013 introduced equal marriage for same-sex couples in England and Wales and recognised such marriages contracted abroad.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Bradford City becomes first UK club to fly the rainbow flag
description: Bradford City, known as the Bantams, became the first UK league football club to fly the rainbow flag at the start of the 2013/14 season, following anti-racist and anti-homophobic work with Unite activist Ann Morgan.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Scotland passes equal marriage into law
description: Equal marriage for same-sex couples was passed into Scottish law in March.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Liberate formed to represent Channel Islands LGBT+ community
description: Liberate, a group representing the LGBT+ community and minorities in the Channel Islands, was established in Guernsey in February and in Jersey in August.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: First Jersey Pride held
description: The first Jersey Pride event took place.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland approves marriage equality by referendum
description: On 22 May, Ireland became the first country in the world to introduce marriage equality by popular vote, with 62.7% in favour. The constitution was amended to state that marriage may be contracted by two persons without distinction as to their sex.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Jersey protects LGBT+ and intersex people from discrimination
description: The Sex and Related Characteristics (Jersey) Regulations 2015 protected LGBT+ and intersex people from discrimination.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland ends religious-school dismissal exemption
description: The Irish government amended the Employment Equality Act 1998 to remove the provision that had allowed religious-run schools to dismiss teachers and staff on the basis of sexual orientation or gender identity.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland allows self-determined legal gender recognition
description: The Gender Recognition Act 2015 allowed legal gender changes in Ireland without medical requirements, on the basis of self-determination for any person aged 18 or over resident and registered in Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TUC adds a plus to its LGBT conference
description: The TUC added a plus to the name of its LGBT conference to ensure all other queer communities were included.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Isle of Man introduces equal marriage
description: Marriage became available to same-sex couples on the Isle of Man.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Policing and Crime Act 2017 pardons convicted gay men
description: The Policing and Crime Act 2017 granted posthumous pardons to gay men in England and Wales convicted under previous laws. Those still living could apply to have their convictions erased, though the process proved difficult.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Isle of Man passes the Equality Act 2017
description: The Isle of Man passed its Equality Act 2017, with much of the law coming into force on 1 January 2019.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Equal marriage introduced across the Channel Islands
description: Equal marriage was introduced in Guernsey from 2017, in Alderney in 2018 and in Sark in 2020.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Irish Taoiseach issues state apology to LGBT+ community
description: On 19 June, Taoiseach Leo Varadkar issued a public apology to members of the LGBT+ community for the suffering and discrimination they faced from the Irish state before the legalisation of homosexuality in 1993.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: TUC calls for equal marriage in Northern Ireland
description: On 9 January the TUC, alongside the National Union of Students, Stonewall and Amnesty International, called for equal marriage in Northern Ireland, which was already in place across the rest of the UK and the Republic of Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Civil partnerships extended to opposite-sex couples in the UK
description: Civil partnerships were extended to opposite-sex couples in England and Wales, then in Scotland, and in Northern Ireland in 2020.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Unite the Union withdraws from London Pride
description: The National LGBT+ committee of Unite voted to end support for London Pride, citing its prioritising of corporate sponsors, a ban on bisexual organisations and a failure to encourage Black and Asian participation. The union favoured regional events and Black Pride instead.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: World Health Organisation declassifies gender dysphoria
description: The World Health Organisation declassified gender dysphoria as a mental illness or disorder.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UNIFI finance union launches with an LGBT network
description: A union for workers in the finance industry, UNIFI, started with an LGBT network for members from the outset.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Scotland to embed LGBT content in the school curriculum
description: Scotland was reported to have become the first country in the world to include LGBT content within the school curriculum. It officially started in October 2021.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Ireland adds gender identity to hate-crime protections
description: The Irish Cabinet formally signed off on protecting trans+ individuals from hate crimes by adding gender identity to the list of protected characteristics, alongside sexual orientation which had been included since 1989.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK government signals plan to ban conversion therapy
description: In the State Opening of Parliament in May, Queen Elizabeth stated that the government planned to ban conversion therapy aimed at altering sexual orientation.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Scottish gender recognition reform blocked by UK government
description: The Gender Recognition Reform (Scotland) Bill passed the Scottish Parliament by 86 votes to 39, but the legislation was blocked by the UK government under section 35 of the Scotland Act 1998.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Jersey extends civil partnerships to opposite-sex couples
description: Jersey extended civil partnerships to opposite-sex couples.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: New UK government pledges to outlaw conversion therapy
description: In the King's Speech, the new Labour government stated its intention to outlaw conversion therapy to protect the whole LGBT+ community.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: UK lifts blood donation restrictions for sexually active men
description: Restrictions on sexually active men giving blood were lifted in the UK.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Northern Ireland court rules gender dysphoria not a mental illness
description: A court ruled that gender dysphoria can no longer be considered a mental illness or disorder. The ruling applies only in Northern Ireland.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024

title: Worker Protection Act 2023 comes into force
description: The Worker Protection Act 2023 came into force on 26 October 2024, requiring employers to take reasonable steps to prevent the sexual harassment of their employees by third parties.

sources.label: Unite the Union — LGBT+ History Timeline 1967-2024
